data.title: Countries May Have To Choose Between China And The U.S.: Implications For Markets
data.description:
Introduction

As the trade war between China and the U.S. intensifies, there might be a world where countries have to choose with whom to trade. This was recently hinted at when a top President Trump official told Europe to choose between U.S. or Chinese communication tech. Additionally, Trump said on Fox News, “We may want countries to choose between the U.S. and China.”

All of this raises the question of what this will mean for markets. In this article, I am going to analyze which side countries will likely choose based on politics and trade connections. Further, I will show you the likely implications of different scenarios for the U.S. and China.

U.S. Trading Partners

Figure 1 shows the U.S. imports by country, while Figure 2 shows U.S. exports. As can be seen, the most important trading partners of the U.S. are: Mexico, Canada, China, Japan, the UK, Vietnam, South Korea, and the EU (while I would like to analyze all partners, this would be far too much for this article).

Starting with Mexico, the country would very likely choose the U.S. as a trading partner for obvious reasons, such as low transport costs and the importance of having a good relationship with its neighboring country. Additionally, the U.S. makes up 76% of exports and 42.8% of imports and is Mexico's biggest trading partner. The same argument works for Canada, which also has the U.S. as its by far most important trading partner.

China itself would, of course, stop trading with the U.S. in the scenario we are discussing. While Japanese exports are roughly equally distributed between China and the U.S., most of their imports come from China. On a political level, however, China and Japan have a long history of hating each other. This would make Japan more likely to side with the US, but due to the economic dependency, Japan will be put in the “undecided but likely U.S.” bucket for our future analysis.

While the UK sells most of its exports to the US, most of its imports come from China. Nevertheless, the gap in exports is much higher, while the UK and the US are allies and share roughly the same political views. Which would mean the UK would pick the US. In the EU, we can find the same trade structure as the UK, with exports going to the US and imports coming from China. Due to the recent tariffs, however, it is not impossible for the EU to choose China, which is why I put them in the “undecided but likely U.S.” bucket, like Japan.

South Korea would likely choose China, due to it being the largest trading partner and more near than the U.S. Nevertheless, it has a better relationship with the U.S., putting it in the “undecided but likely China” bucket. This brings us to Vietnam as the last important U.S. trading partner. They come in the bucket “undecided,” mainly because they are dependent on both partners.

Chinese Trading Partners

Figures 3 and 4 show the largest trading partners of China by imports and exports. Apart from the countries already discussed, important countries are: Australia, Russia, Brazil, Hong Kong, India, and Indonesia.

Russia and Hong Kong would pick China for obvious reasons. India comes in the “undecided” bucket, as it has important trade relations with both countries. Meanwhile, Indonesia would pick China as it is too dependent on the country in terms of trade. Brazil would very likely pick China as well for the same reason. Australia, surprisingly, would come into the “undecided but likely China” bucket. While it has strong ties to the U.S., the economic dependency could be too high for the country.

Analysis

Figure 5 shows a table listing all the named countries, their bucket, and their % of U.S. and Chinese exports and imports. This allows us now to build scenarios for the U.S. and China. The first scenario would be the standard scenario, meaning every country joins the country it is likely to join. For the completely undecided countries, the average will be taken. In this scenario, the U.S. would lose roughly 25% of its imports and 18% of its export partners. Using the same scenario for China, the country would lose roughly 35% of its import partners, while not exporting to 46% of its former export partners. While the base scenario would already be terrible for the U.S., China would be hit far harder. Interestingly, most of the pain would be based on whom the EU decided to trade with, as it makes up a lot of both countries' imports and exports.

In a best-case scenario, where every country decides for the U.S. that is not fully decided, the U.S. would lose 16.5% of its import partners and 11.5% of its export partners, while they would lose a tremendous amount of 55% of import and 43% of export partners in the worst-case scenario, where every undecided country would choose China.

In contrast, China would lose 10.6% of its import partners and 21.3% of its export partners in the best case. Additionally, they would lose 50.5% of imports and 56.8% of export partners in a worst-case scenario.

All of this shows the tremendous impact of countries having to choose with whom to trade.

Implications

So, what does this mean? Interestingly, it means that the EU is in a great position to make a deal at the moment. Losing the EU as a trading partner is something neither China nor the U.S. can allow themselves. This is also a reason why the scenario just analyzed is unlikely to happen, as long as a country is not 100% sure to have the support of the EU.

While most of the analysis looked worse for China than for the U.S., it would be terrible for both economies and currencies. While companies would earn far fewer profits and lose some of their supply chains, the currencies of both countries would be far less used and likely to crash. A solution for investors to hedge against such a scenario could be to short the Chinese currency, as it will likely be devalued anyway to counteract some of the effects of tariffs and increase economic activity.

data.title: Huntington Bancshares Incorporated 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Huntington Bancshares Incorporated in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: Huntington Bancshares: Back To An Attractive Income Opportunity
data.description:
Today we continue our Q1 regional bank coverage with Huntington Bancshares Incorporated (NASDAQ:HBAN). We cover this stock every quarter, and our last take was suggesting you consider taking some profit of $17-$18 and looking to come back in sub-$14. With the market and sector selloff, we are back to these levels again, and we think you do some buying for income here, as the yield is an attractive 4.7%. Consider scaling in, buying in 50 cent blocks, from here down to $12 and then hold for income, Let us discuss the just-reported Q1 earnings.

Huntington Bancshares Q1 earnings headline performance

On the headline results, Huntington Bancshares exceeded expectations. The bank reported Q1 net income of $527 million, down $3 million from the sequential quarter, but better than expected. It was up a strong $108 million, or 26% from last year.

Folks this is a tremendous result. Adjusted earnings per common share were $0.34, flat from the sequential quarter, but were up $0.08 from a year ago.

Both loans and deposits see growth in Q1

We like a buy for income here because loans and total deposits continue to grow reliably. The bank is executing well. The best banks, the ones you want to own, grow loans and deposits and maintain strong asset quality. Huntington fits the bill.

Here in Q1, average total deposits increased $2.1 billion, or 1%, compared to the sequential Q4. These deposits were also up $10.9 billion, or 7%, from last year's Q1. On top of that, loan balances continue to grow. Average total loans and leases increased $2.7 billion (or 2%), from the sequential quarter to $130.9 billion. Compared to a year ago, these loans were up $8.9 billion, or 7%. So, while other regional banks we have covered so far have shown some weakness, we are not seeing it here with Huntington.

If the economy meaningfully worsens, we could see this change, but for now, activity remains strong. We also want to highlight that net interest margin increased another 7 basis points to 3.10% from 3.03% last quarter. This is very strong, and long term, the bank has enjoyed a higher net interest margin on average relative to peers.

Huntington Bancshares asset quality improves

Asset quality metrics improved in Q1. Net charge-offs were just 0.26% of average total loans and leases for the quarter, and this was a 4 basis point improvement from 0.30% in Q4. We continue to watch this very critical metric, and this decline is encouraging, as charge-offs are pretty stable while loans are going up.

That said, the allowance for credit losses has improved on a percentage basis for all loans as well. These are at $2.5 billion, or 1.87%, of total loans and leases at quarter end, a 1 basis point improvement. Finally, efficiency has always been strong here. Earlier in 2024, we opined that we expected the ratio to be back under 60%. We saw in Q3 2024 it was 59.4%, and 58.6% in Q4. It remained strong at 58.9% in Q1. This is a solid performance.

Huntington Bancshares valuation reset

We are seeing strong performance, but we have a new valuation here after the market selloff. From $18 to $13 offers an opportunity and is valued in our opinion for a quality buy here. We are back to a relatively high yield, trading at just 9.6X forward earnings and 1.5X tangible book value.

Final thoughts

This is a well-run bank, and shares are now a buy. We like scaling in, buying in 50 cent blocks here and down to $12. This will give you an average basis with over a 5% yield to hold for income.

data.title: Avanos Medical: Cheap, But With Caveats
data.description:
Today, we put Avanos Medical, Inc. (NYSE:AVNS) in the spotlight for the first time. This medical device concern named a new CEO in March that came over from Siemens Medical Solutions USA. The stock is also cheap on a P/E ratio, after falling nearly 25% year to date. Especially compared to the overall market where the S&P 500 (SP500) is trading just over 21 times forward earnings currently. Is the stock in the bargain bin? An analysis follows below.

Avanos Medical is headquartered in Alpharetta, GA. The company operates in many healthcare segments and sells primarily to hospitals, third-party distributors, healthcare facilities and other healthcare providers. These include offers digestive health products including neonatal feeding offerings, non-opioid pain solutions, including surgical pain and recovery products as well as radiofrequency ablation products to treat chronic pain conditions and knee osteoarthritis hyaluronic acid pain relief injection products. The stock currently trades at around $12.00 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of $550 million.

The company has two main divisions Enteral Feeding and Pain Management & Recovery. The new CEO joints Avanos Medical as the company was already going through some transition. The company sold its struggling Respiratory Health business to SunMed Group in the summer of 2023. Around the same time, Avanos purchased Toronto-based Diros Technology Inc. with its radiofrequency products portfolio.

Of note, in the fourth quarter, Avanos finished all their obligations to perform certain manufacturing services in connection with the sale of its Respiratory Health business. All assets that were previously accounted for as 'held for sale' were officially transferred to SunMed on October 1st, 2024. In addition, in January the company received notification from the DOJ that Avanos met their commitments with regard to their deferred prosecution agreement. Avanos will pay a $22 million fine for misbranding its Microcool surgical gowns.

Recent Results:

Avanos Medical posted its Q4 numbers on February 26th. The company delivered non-GAAP of 43 cents a share, three pennies a share above expectations. Gross margin fell slightly to 54.6% from 54.8% in the same period a year ago.

Adjusted EBITDA fell to $28.6 million from $32.1 million. Adjusted EBITDA for FY2024 for continuing operations was $108 million. On a GAAP basis, Avanos took a non-cash impairment charge of $437 million. This was based upon an assessment that the fair value of Avanos' single company reporting unit was below the unit's carrying value. For FY2024, Avanos booked non-GAAP earnings of $1.35 a share, up 31% from FY2023.

Revenues rose 3.6% on a year-over-year to $179.6 million, beating expectations by some $2 million. Enteral Feeding drove the revenue increase as sales grew to $106 million from $94 million in the same period a year ago. Pain Management sales fell to $74 million from $77 million.

Management provided the following guidance for FY2025.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

Avanos Medical gets little coverage from Wall Street currently. Since the company's fourth quarter results hit the wires, both JMP Securities and Stifel Nicolaus ($17 price target, down from $20 previously) maintained Hold ratings on the stock. That is the only analyst firm commentary I can find on Avanos Medical in 2025.

Just under two percent of the outstanding float in the shares are currently held short. One company officer sold just over $105,000 worth of stock in March. That is the only insider activity in the stock year to date. Several insiders sold approximately $200,000 worth of equity in 2024, collectively. There was no insider activity in the shares 2021 through 2023.

Avanos ended the fiscal 2024 with a net debt of $27 million after starting the year with a net debt of just over $80 million. The company produced $82.9 million in free cash flow in FY2024, of which $53.1 million came in Q4. It should be noted that $18 million of free cash came from a tax receivable. Avanos announced a $25 million stock buyback authorization program in late November. The restructuring efforts of the past two years have been a key driver of improved cash flow.

Conclusion:

Avanos Medical made $1.35 a share in FY2024 on just under $678 million of revenues. The current analyst firm consensus has profits falling to $1.12 a share as sales decrease slightly to $673 million in FY2025. They project earnings rebounding to $1.27 a share on three to four percent revenue growth in FY2026.

The company has done a commendable job in bringing down leverage and substantially improving free cash flow. The stock is not expensive at nine times trailing earnings. The shares do not pay a dividend and are thinly traded, it should be noted. There is little liquidity in the options market against the equity, taking a covered call strategy off the table.

In addition, both sales and earnings are projected to decline in FY2025. While the company does not break down international sales, it does sell globally. Avanos also sources products from Mexico, Canada, and China. Therefore, there are significant tariff policy unknowns for the company which management referenced in its fourth quarter conference call.

Given all of this, I have Avanos on my "watch list," but don't plan to take a stake in it until the company returns to growth and tariff impacts are more known.

data.title: Down 20%, Still A Buy: Why I'm Doubling Down On UnitedHealth Group
data.description:
Thesis Summary

UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (NYSE:UNH) is down 20% after a Q1 earnings miss and lowered guidance. But is this selloff justified?

The company is undoubtedly dealing with some issues, but this is, in my opinion, a market overreaction.

The stock is now back where it was a month ago, and I believe this is a good time to buy.

I remain bullish on the back of the company’s compelling defensive revenue streams, earnings forecasts, and favorable valuation.

UNH continues to be a very solid company, a good defensive buy to own in hard times, even if today it doesn’t seem like it.

I rate UNH a Strong Buy after the selloff.

Q1 Overview

UNH posted decent earnings, with revenue up 10% YoY and adjusted EPS of $7.20. Operating earnings were $9.1/share.

However, the company’s full-year guidance was lowered. The company now expects full-year EPS of around $26.

This is due to higher expected costs associated with Medicare Advantage care activity and OptumHealth reimbursement headwinds.

Still, we saw the company add 780,000 members YTD and Optum Health expects to serve 650,000 more “value-based care patients” by year-end.

It’s worth noting that Optum Revenue was down sequentially, while UNH revenue saw a strong increase of 18%.

While UNH is the “traditional” insurance business, Optum is a health service that looks to serve patients through data analytics and technology.

Why I Still Like UNH

I think this is a great and rare opportunity to buy a quality stock at a favorable price.

Defensive Leader

UNH is a market leader in the healthcare sector, which is a defensive sector that does well even in recessions. The risk of a recession or even stagflation has dramatically risen in the last few months, which is why defensive stocks, like UNH, have actually outperformed recently.

While the earnings change the short-term outlook, the long-term appeal doesn’t change. Healthcare is resilient to downturns and even inflation as it provides a necessary service.

Growth And Stability

In this regard, UNH offers the best of both worlds, with steady revenue from UNH and some high-growth potential from Optum.

The segment launched in 2011 breaks down into three divisions, Optum Health, Optum Insight and Optum RX.

In my opinion, it allows UNH to capture an underserved market, while leveraging new technologies like AI.

There’s clearly a demand, with Optum Insight boasting a $32.9B backlog.

On top of that, these revenues have higher margins than the insurance business.

Historically Undervalued

Based on historical levels, UNH is now undervalued. With a P/E of around 17, this is below the 10-year average of 20 and the PEG ratio is now under 1.5, also compelling.

Ultimately, if you believe that current estimates are roughly accurate, projecting double-digit growth, then this is a great investment.

Add to these buybacks, dividends and a strong balance sheet, and you have a great long-term investment.

Technical Analysis

From a technical perspective, the price action in the coming days following the earnings will be very interesting.

UNH is set to open below the 200-week EMA around $465. This level has been broken a couple of times in the last few years, but the dip hasn’t lasted long.

Will this time be the exception?

We have some support around the previous low at $460, but below that could open the doors to some pain.

It will certainly be very interesting to see how different indicators like the RSI and MACD look in the coming week.

Risks

Of course, the recent earnings have given us reason for concern. Margins have been squeezed due to rising medical costs, especially in Medicare Advantage. Will this trend persist?

On the other hand, there’s definitely some uncertainty and risk related to policy, such as changes in Medicaid. This administration is certainly not afraid of shaking things up.

Final Thoughts

In conclusion, the current selloff in UNH seems overblown, since it follows only a small earnings miss. There could be a combination of factors at play here, such as the fact that the stock was already overstretched, and perhaps increased overall fear and volatility in the market.

But that doesn’t change the fact that UNH is a strong defensive play, which will, in fact, help hedge your portfolio from an economic downturn.

data.title: Elisa Oyj 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Elisa Oyj in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: Kohl's Is As Cheap As The Looks Of Its Stores
data.description:
Introduction

Kohl's Corporation (NYSE:KSS) is a major department store chain that operates around 1,170 stores across most of the U.S. Kohl’s offers a mix of affordable goods, such as apparel, home appliances, and others, targeting the middle class.

The company has faced major competition from e-commerce and other discount retailers like Amazon, TJ Maxx, and Ross. Kohl's has also faced some operational setbacks as it is quite hard to balance physical store and digital growth, as shown by the 41.87% short interest. Furthermore, the company is and will be heavily impacted by the current U.S.-China trade war, which leaves some questions about where the legacy chain can still fit in today's market.

I want to see if Kohl’s could be a big turnaround story for gutsy investors or if the company is doomed to fail like other retailers such as Bed Bath & Beyond or Big Lots.

Current Difficulties and Market Dynamics

Kohl’s has now gone through 3 years of declining sales, as the company's core categories, apparel and footwear, are currently underperforming. Middle and lower-income customers are choosing more and more value and essentials compared to the pre-pandemic era, due to inflation and the resulting rise in living costs. These demographics are particularly sensitive to price increases. The shift has reduced demand for even Kohl’s private label, which lacks the pricing edge of The TJX Companies, Inc. (TJX). This is reflected by the Consumer Confidence Index, which has been hovering lower and lower in 2025.

The company also chose to modernize its apparel offering to boost its main segment, but that has not resonated with most of its customers. Growth areas like Sephora and Babies “R” Us are helping to boost sales, but are still not offsetting the declines from the company's core segments. In fact, measures like Sephora shop-in-shops, which are aimed at attracting younger customers, had the effect of alienating its core middle-aged and older demographics, which are already spending less due to the current macroeconomic backdrop. The home category and digital sales have also been lackluster to say the least, adding to the overall condition of the company.

To respond to some of these issues, the company is closing 27 underperforming stores across 15 different states, around 3% of its total locations. It is also closing the San Bernardino E-commerce fulfillment center, thanks to improved in-store capabilities. Furthermore, Kohl’s will cut its corporate workforce by around 10% in order to boost profitability, which is significantly trailing its peers.

Now we need to look at how the current challenges posed by the administration, with the proposed tariffs on the world (with a current 90-day pause), but also the ever-escalating tariffs between China and America. Kohl’s, like many department chains, sources the majority of its inventory from cheap Chinese manufacturers for its private-label, and indirectly, all the key brands such as Nike that are purchased in-store have manufacturing in China. Kohl’s has close to 0 wiggle room to pass on costs to customers amid a 10% broad tariff, let alone 125% tariffs for China. Furthermore, competitors like TJX and Ross Stores, Inc. (ROST) have more flexible sourcing and greater pricing power as they lie at the bottom of the market in terms of prices. Walmart and Amazon also have a more diversified supply chain and a wider scale, rendering the two companies less exposed than Kohl’s. To be fair, KSS is already looking at different strategies to mitigate the tariffs' effects, such as suppliers from Mexico or South America, but these will require big investments when the company is already in the doldrums.

2025 outlook

The company is targeting to offer a more balanced assortment for customers and regain traction in lost categories such as jewelry and legacy homes, and build on momentum for areas like Sephora. KSS is also enhancing its omnichannel platform, such as trip assurance, and optimizing store layouts. To be honest, I’m not sure if the company can substantially enhance its position with these measures, as the issues that Kohl’s is facing are more systemic.

Although its new CEO, Ashley Buchanan, seems promising with his vast experience in retail with jobs at Walmart and Michaels, and his rhetoric that Kohl’s is built on strong foundations. Buchanan wants to regain Kohl’s dynamism with the momentum of Sephora’s success.

Looking at the numbers, the company expects net sales to decline further by 5%/7% in 2025 compared to 2024, while the operating margin stays in a range of 2.2% to 2.6%. Diluted EPS for the full year should range between $0.1 to $0.6, with a CAPEX of around $400 million for the year, lower than the last 3 previous years.

Valuation

Kohl’s, like the look of its shops, is fundamentally cheap, with the lowest P/TB that I’ve seen in a while. The company receives an A+ score in valuation according to Seeking Alpha, with metrics such as its 6.39 P/E GAAP, which is 61.4% lower than the sector median. The company's EV/EBITDA is also in that range, at 6.22. Now the star of this value case would be the company’s book value (or tangible book value as they’re the same in this case), standing at $34.25, giving KSS a P/B of 0.18. This comes mainly from the company’s property portfolio that has a net value of $9.67B, with Plants & Equipment and also inventories worth $2.95B. Kohl’s properties have been a talking point to justify the undervaluation case for a while now, as Macellum Advisors pushed the company to monetize its real estate back in 2022, and Oak Street made a $1.5-$2B bid for a portion of the assets. This came from the back of various firms offering to buy the company, like Sycamore’s proposal of at least $65 a share or Franchise Group offering $69 for a $9B total offer, a far cry from the current $700m market cap. Here’s how the company compares to its peers in terms of valuation metrics:

Now the cheap fundamentals can be explained from the two following graphs that would directly make any value investor gasp:

Debt is rising for Kohl’s as sales and bottom-line margins are declining quarter after quarter. Fitch has recently downgraded KSS’s debt to BB- with a negative outlook due to the assessment of ongoing operational challenges and concerns about the timing of business stabilization. Most long-term corporate bonds are priced well below par, ranging from $49.5 to $62.2, letting us know that the credit market has a lack of faith in the future prospects for the company. Kohl’s also plans on refinancing its July 25 debt, which will certainly raise its interest expenses with the deterioration of the business, the subsequent rating downgrades, and higher interest rates.

And while all department stores are having issues with the current market dynamics, Kohl's has some of the worst profitability metrics to sustain the downturn, especially compared to TJX or ROSS.

Conclusion

Due to the structural dynamics facing Kohl's, I cannot rate the company as a buy, as it is certainly impossible to say if the business is sustainable in the long term. It is undeniable that the company is cheap, based on any metrics, especially considering that the company has inventories worth close to $3B when the market cap is only $700m. A possible buyout is certainly possible thanks to the already-mentioned large asset base. However, it would be foolish to only base any investment on this particular scenario. In this regard, I would rate Kohl's as a hold, and that's for gutsy, high-risk investors.

data.title: MAGS' Selloff Is Here - Fundamentals Remain Robust No Matter The Tariffs
data.description:
MAGS Is Even More Attractively Valued After The Recent Selloff - Reiterating Buy

We previously covered Roundhill Magnificent Seven ETF (NASDAQ:MAGS) in January 2025, discussing why it remained a compelling ETF Buy for investors seeking high growth through the Magnificent 7 stocks, thanks to their robust long-term prospects through the ongoing cloud super cycle, growing e-commerce demand, and market leading smartphone/ EV sales.

Combined with the relatively reasonable valuations and improved margin of safety after the correction from December 2024 peaks, we had initiated a Buy rating upon a moderate retracement to its 100 day moving averages of $49/ $50 ranges, as per historical trends in April/ August 2024.

MAGS 1Y ETF Price

Since then, our recommended Buy point has materialized indeed, with the added bonus of $40.50 entry points during the worst of the April 2025 selloff, before MAGS rapidly recovers to the $44 ranges.

Much of the headwinds are naturally attributed to the bursting AI bubble by mid-February 2025, worsened by the intensified tariff/ trade war and the higher recessionary risks of up to 79%, with it already triggering the deep market wide sell off by -19% between the 52-week heights and the recent bottom.

For now, we can understand why MAGS and other ETFs have yet to recover to previous heights, especially given the rapidly developing tariff war, the new US administration's 145% tariffs imposed on Chinese imports, and the Chinese's 125% tariffs on US imports.

These developments matter indeed, since there is likely to be great impact on three of MAGS' holdings, including Apple (AAPL), Nvidia (NVDA), and Tesla (TSLA).

Given that China comprises 17.1% (-1.8 points YoY) of AAPL's FY2024 revenues, 13.1% of (-3.8 points YoY) of NVDA's FY2025 revenues, 21.4% (-1 points YoY) of TSLA's FY2024 revenues, we are unsurprised about that these stocks have had extremely painful price correction by -33.7%, -38.4%, and -54.5% between the 52 week heights and the recent bottom.

This is especially since the new US administration has already highlighted the temporary nature of the recent electronics exemption, with it apparent that there may be more volatility in the near-term, pending further policy clarity and/ or any resolution (unlikely in our opinion).

The same pessimism has also been observed in AAPL's decision to "chartered cargo flights to ferry 600 tons of iPhones, or as many as 1.5 million, to the United States from India, after it stepped up production there in an effort to beat President Donald Trump's tariffs," with a similar build up in inventory trend also observed in the semiconductor chips sector.

If anything, TSLA has had to stop taking "new orders for Model S and Model X vehicles on its Chinese website" as well, worsened by the intensifying domestic competition and the deteriorating public opinion surrounding the CEO.

These headwinds perhaps contributed to the automaker's underwhelming Q1'25 deliveries of 336.68K vehicles (-32% QoQ/ -12.9% YoY), compared to a year ago at -20.1% QoQ/ -8.5% YoY and two years ago at +4.3% QoQ/ +36.3% YoY.

With these remaining early days in the uncertain macroeconomic environment, we believe that these three stocks are likely to remain impacted ahead, albeit well balanced by the robust prospects observed in MAGS's three other holdings, aside from Amazon (AMZN).

For example, Alphabet (GOOG) remains the undisputed global search engine leader at a market share at 89.7% as of March 2025, with the segment directly responsible for $198.08B in FY2024 revenues (+13.1% YoY).

This is on top of its improving YouTube and Cloud monetization, as observed in the leading streaming share at 11.6% as of March 2025 (+1.9 points YoY) and the cloud computing share at 12% in Q4'24 (+1 points YoY), along with the FY2024 revenue growth to $36.14B (+14.6% YoY) and $43.22B (+30.6% YoY), respectively.

While the AI bubble may have burst by mid February 2025, readers must note that GOOG has already "reaffirmed the company pledge to invest about $75B to build out data center infrastructure."

Combined with the rich remaining performance obligations (primarily related to Google Cloud) of $93.2B (+25.7% YoY), we can understand the management's conviction surrounding its bright long-term prospects as a cloud provider.

The same has been observed for Meta (META), thanks to its leading social media market share at 78.83% as of March 2025 (+4.94 points YoY) and growing global daily active users at 3.35B in FQ4'24 (+5% YoY).

It is unsurprising then, that META has been able to report rich FY2024 advertising revenues at $160.61B (+21.7% YoY) and net incomes of $62.33B (+59.5% YoY), with its social media/ advertising business model likely to be well insulated no matter the uncertain macroeconomic environment.

Microsoft's (MSFT) enterprise business model appears to be resilient as well, attributed to its strategic subscription model.

The same has been observed in the growing Productivity and Business Processes (enterprise) annualized revenues of $117.72B (+13.8% YoY) and the commercial multi-year remaining performance obligations of $298B (+34.2% YoY).

This is on top of the robust cloud opportunities with 21% in global market share (-3 points YoY) and the growing adoption of Microsoft Cloud at revenues of $40.9B (+21% YoY), no matter the temporary PC demand headwinds.

On the one hand, AMZN's commerce opportunities may have temporarily been impacted attributed to the "cancelled orders that originate from China" and the consequently, higher selling prices attributed to the tariffs. However, it goes without saying that all other US-based retailers, including Walmart (WMT), Target (TGT), and Costco (COST) are likely to be impacted across the board.

On the other hand, readers must note that commerce only comprises 42% of AMZN's FY2024 sales (-1.8 points YoY), with most of its high growth prospects currently tied to the:

  • advertising segment at 8.8% of FY2024 revenues with +19.8% YoY growth,
  • subscription services at 6.9%/ +10.3% YoY, and
  • AWS segment at 16.8%/ +18.5% YoY.

This is especially since AWS comprises 58% of AMZN's FY2024 operating incomes (-8.8 points YoY), while reporting robust bottom-line growth at +61.7% YoY - with it underscoring why AMZN may still be able to generate robust financial metrics despite the temporary commerce headwinds.

Lastly, we must highlight that the US comprises 49% of GOOG's FY2024 revenues (+2 points YoY), 43.7% of META's FY2025 revenues (-1.6 points YoY), 50.8% of MSFT's FY2024 revenues (+0.5 points YoY), and 68.6% of AMZN's FY2024 revenues (-0.2 points YoY), with it somewhat tempering the potential headwinds from the ongoing tariff war with China.

This is especially since there has yet to be a tariff imposed on software/ advertising opportunities, with these four MAGS stocks' performance likely to well balance the headwinds observed in the hardware-focused MAGS stocks, as discussed above.

So, Is MAGS ETF A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

MAGS 2Y ETF Price

For now, the bulls have quickly defended MAGS' recent meltdown at the established August 2024/ April 2025 bottom of $40 ranges, as the ETF bounces to its next support levels of $44 ranges.

Thanks to the selloff, the ETF is now cheaper at PE ratio of 33.13x compared to the recent heights of 52x/ 10Y median of NA, with a similar conclusion also observed against its semiconductor/ tech-focused ETF peers, including:

  • VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) at 48.70x/ 35.60x/ 22.40x,
  • Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ) at 32.81x/ 33.80x/ 25.70x,
  • SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (SPY) at 25.20x/ 33.00x/ 20.60x, respectively.

MAGS' Money Flow Index & Williams %R Momentum

While it is apparent that we have missed MAGS' bottom, based on the recovering Money Flow Index & Williams %R Momentum indexes above (in red oval), we believe that the ETF is still attractive at current levels.

This is especially since the Mag7 stocks remains reasonably valued compared to historical trends, including:

  • AAPL at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 27.89x against the recent heights of 35.03x and the 5Y mean of 28.61x,
  • AMZN at 28.85x/ 41.17x/ 175.55x,
  • GOOG at 18.01x/ 23.91x/ 25.36x,
  • NVDA at 24.45x/ 35.58x/ 47.70x,
  • META at 21.21x/ 29.10x/ 23.53x,
  • MSFT at 29.46x/ 34.05x/ 31.80x, and
  • TSLA at 98.89x/ 153.93x/ 116.26x, respectively.

As a result of the improved margin of safety from the ongoing selloff, the moderating CBOE Volatility Index, and the robust bullish support at the $40 ranges during the worst of the April 2025 selloff, we are reiterating our Buy rating for the MAGS ETF here.

Do not miss this dip.

data.title: Fastly Outlook: Significant Progress Needed For Catalyst (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Outlook

Fastly, Inc. (NYSE:FSLY) provides a range of content delivery and related security services for websites worldwide.

I previously wrote about FSLY in October 2020 with a Hold outlook due to difficult growth comparables in a challenging 2020 pandemic environment.

More recently, the company has faced dwindling revenue from large customers and needs to further broaden its customer base while making progress toward operating breakeven.

My outlook on FSLY is a bearish Sell for the near term until it can show significant progress across several areas.

Fastly’s Markets And Approach

The primary revenue source for Fastly is via its Network Services segment, which provides a content delivery network and dynamic site acceleration service offering.

The company also provides related security services through a web application firewall and various other DDoS protection and bot management services.

These capabilities are broadly part of the Infrastructure-as-a-Service [IaaS] industry.

According to a 2025 market research report by Fortune Business Insights, the IaaS industry was an estimated $157 billion in 2024 and is expected to exceed $712 billion by 2032.

If achieved, this growth would represent a whopping CAGR of 20.8% from 2025 to 2032.

The primary drivers for growth are an increasing need to simplify backup solutions and for edge computing acceleration and protection due to hybrid cloud computing environments.

The chart below shows the historical and expected future growth trajectory of the North American IaaS market through 2032:

Fastly’s sales model is a combination of a free self-serve trial portal, direct field sales efforts to enterprises, and its Global Partner Network of resellers and managed service providers.

Its core pricing model is a consumption-based model, but in late 2023, the company provided a "no-overage" flat-rate bundle option, and it provides custom pricing for special situations.

Fastly’s closest competitors would be Cloudflare (NET), Akamai Technologies (AKAM) and Amazon’s CloudFront (AMZN).

Below is a breakdown of the firm’s operating segments by percentage revenue contribution:

Recent Financial Trends

Total revenue by quarter (columns) has risen due to market share gains and large customer retention, although net revenue retention declined sequentially and YoY to 102%, a negative signal. Operating income by quarter (red line) has remained heavily negative because of lower gross margin and increasing sales & marketing investments.

The degree of operating leverage has trended slightly lower over time, with greater variability in recent quarters:

Gross profit margin by quarter (blue line) has dropped recently as a result of higher international bandwidth costs. Selling and G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue by quarter (orange line) have trended lower due to restructuring of selling activities to focus on larger logos and increasing upsell opportunities. R&D expenses as a function of revenue (purple line) have trended lower as the company has sought to reign in costs.

Earnings per share (Diluted) have remained negative with little consistent or meaningful move toward breakeven:

(All data in the above charts is GAAP.)

For balance sheet results, FSLY ended the quarter with $295.9 million in cash, equivalents and short-term investment and $337.6 million in total debt, all of which is long-term.

Over the trailing twelve months, free cash flow was only $5.3 million, and capital expenditures were $11.1 million.

The company paid a whopping $107.9 million in stock-based compensation, or SBC, in the last four quarters, so free cash flow net of SBC was negative ($102.6 million)

SBC as a percentage of revenue over the trailing twelve-month period was an extremely high 76.7%.

The net dollar decrease in stock outstanding for the last four quarters was valued at $2.1 million.

In the past 12 months, FSLY’s stock price has fallen by 58% vs. that of competitors Cloudflare’s rise of 20% and Akamai’s drop of 27%, as the chart indicates below.

Below is a handy major financial and operating metrics table for Fastly is shown here:

Metric

Amount

EV/Sales - FWD

(Source - Seeking Alpha.)

The company's Rule of 40 performance is show in the table here, with only moderate topline revenue growth dragged down by still heavily negative operating margin:

Rule of 40 Performance (Unadjusted)

Q4 2024

Revenue Growth %

7.5%

Operating Margin

-24.1%

Total

(Source - Seeking Alpha.)

Why I’m Bearish On Fastly

Fastly’s topline revenue growth rate expectations are lower in 2025 than 2024’s results and net revenue retention rates have dropped significantly year-over-year, from 113% to 102%, both negative signals.

While management appears to be reacting to these developments by focusing on upselling, that is quite an NRR drop in a single year.

With its focus on larger, higher-tier customers, Fastly produces slower customer acquisition and is primarily centered on key metropolitan areas throughout its network.

The company has more of a narrow, niche offering and a smaller global footprint than competitors and execution has hampered its growth in recent years.

FSLY also has significant customer concentration risk, with its top 10 customers accounting for low 30% of its total revenue.

It continues to benefit from remaining U.S. TikTok traffic, but such traffic is under a cloud of uncertainty as the fate of TikTok's ownership hangs in the balance.

I’ve prepared a graphic illustrating the frequency of various negative-oriented keyword terms in the most recent conference call.

The chart shows the company is experiencing revenue headwind pressures from some of its largest customers.

As to valuation, I’ve compared Fastly to much larger Akamai for various major metrics, shown in the table here:

Metric

(Source - Seeking Alpha.)

The comparison highlights that the market is currently valuing Fastly at a much lower forward EV/Sales multiple despite a higher expected forward revenue growth rate.

Also, Fastly’s EV/Sales multiple has experienced more than 50% compression over the past year, as the chart shows here:

Looking ahead, management seeks to broaden the company's customer base so it is less concentrated among large customers.

While the company has made progress in its revenue diversification mix, it will need to generate more growth from its security, compute and observability offerings, requiring growing its investment in those areas.

Revenue retention has also been a major drag on performance and although management said it expects it to improve in H2 2025, I’m in wait-and-see mode.

For now, my opinion is to deploy capital elsewhere until FSLY proves that it can execute in multiple areas at the same time, so my outlook is a bearish Sell on Fastly in the near term.

data.title: FXU: Utilities Dashboard For April
data.description:
This monthly article series reports industry metrics, aiming at a top-down analysis of the utilities sector. It may also help analyze sector ETFs such as Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU) and First Trust Utilities AlphaDEX Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:FXU), whose largest holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italics describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each industry: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or unavailable when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively as EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages over a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for hardware in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in hardware companies.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between two valuation ratios (EY, SY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh). FY is reported for consistency with other sector dashboards, but it is ignored in utilities’ score to avoid some inconsistencies. In the same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the two valuation ratios are of equal importance.

Current data

The next table shows the metrics and scores as of writing. Columns stand for all the data defined above.

Value and Quality chart

The next chart plots the Value and Quality Scores by industry. Higher is better.

Evolution since last month

The most notable change is a deterioration of quality score in water and gas utilities.

Momentum

The next chart plots median returns by subsector.

Interpretation

Water utilities are undervalued by about 13% relative to 11-year averages, while gas, electric and multi utilities are moderately overvalued. Overvaluation may be partly offset by a good quality score for electric and multi utilities.

Focus on FXU

First Trust Utilities AlphaDEX Fund ETF was launched on 5/8/2007 and tracks the StrataQuant® Utilities Index. FXU has 40 holdings, a 30-day SEC yield of 2.69%, and an expense ratio of 0.63%, while the capital weighted ETF XLU charges only 0.09%. As described by First Trust, the underlying index ranks, selects and weights utilities stocks of the Russell 1000 Index separately based on growth and value metrics. It results in a portfolio holding stocks with either growth or value characteristics.

The portfolio is quite concentrated, but risks related to individual companies are moderate. Indeed, the top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 39.6% of asset value, and the heaviest position weighs 4.2%. FXU is more diversified than XLU, whose largest position (NextEra Energy) weighs 11.7%.

FXU is more diversified than XLU (40 and 31 holdings, respectively), but it doesn’t make a significant difference in historical return and risk-adjusted performance. These funds are equivalents for long-term shareholders. However, XLU has much higher trading volumes, making it a better trading instrument. Investors seeking a more balanced fund may prefer Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Utilities ETF (RSPU). Additionally, RSPU has a lower fee (0.40%) and has outperformed FXU since its inception.

Dashboard List

I use the first table to calculate value and quality scores. It may also be used in a stock-picking process to check how companies stand among their peers. For example, the EY column tells us that a gas company with an Earnings Yield above 0.0550 (or price/earnings below 18.18) is in the better half of the industry regarding this metric. A Dashboard List is sent every month to Quantitative Risk & Value subscribers, with the most profitable companies standing in the better half among their peers regarding the three valuation metrics at the same time. The stocks below are part of the list sent to subscribers a few weeks ago.

It is a rotational model with a statistical bias toward excess returns on the long-term, not the result of in-depth stock analysis.
